one_line_takeaway: VLM jailbreaks are not a failure to perceive harm but a distinct, linearly-separable internal representation state reachable via an image-induced shift; removing the projection of that shift onto a learned 'jailbreak direction' at inference time (JRS-Rem) cuts ASR by up to ~65-72 points on LLaVA-1.5-7B/HADES with near-zero utility cost, generalizing across explicit, implicit, and adversarial jailbreak scenarios though less reliably on InternVL-Chat-19B.
key_finding: On LLaVA-1.5-7B with HADES, JRS-Rem cuts ASR from 77.3/73.7/84.9 (SD/TYPO/SD+TYPO, no defense) to 12.2/9.40/12.4, i.e. absolute reductions of 65.1/64.3/72.5 points -- the largest reduction of any baseline. Across explicitly harmful scenarios (Table 2), JRS-Rem is best-or-second-best for all 3 VLMs x 3 datasets x 3 image types. It also generalizes to implicitly harmful inputs (Table 3: HADES ASR 67.0->35.3, MM-SafetyBench 67.2->19.1 for LLaVA-1.5-7B) and to adversarial attacks it was never tuned on (Table 4: LLaVA-1.5-7B MML-M ASR 67.8->9.0, gradient-attack ASR 76.1->11.3). On utility benchmarks (Table 5), JRS-Rem is near loss-free for LLaVA-1.5-7B: MM-Vet 32.1->31.6, ScienceQA 64.0->64.0, MME 1754.9->1754.9, versus baselines like CMRM which drop MME by 1075.1 points and AdaShield which drops ScienceQA by 24.5 points. Pairwise cosine similarities of jailbreak directions across 9 dataset/image-type combinations exceed 0.7, and AUROC for the direction separating jailbreak vs. refusal exceeds 0.85 in middle/deep layers, supporting the claim that a single, stable jailbreak direction generalizes across data distributions.
core_contribution: The paper studies VLM (vision-language model) jailbreaks using explicitly harmful text prompts (as opposed to prior work's implicitly harmful prompts where harmful intent is conveyed only via the image). Using PCA visualization, cosine-distance-to-centroid analysis, and linear probing on last-token representations of LLaVA-1.5-7B, ShareGPT4V-7B, and InternVL-Chat-19B, the authors show that (1) benign vs. harmful inputs are linearly separable in representation space, and (2) among harmful inputs, jailbreak and refusal samples also form separable clusters, and (3) a large fraction of jailbreak responses contain 'safety warning' language (keyword-matched) even when the model ultimately complies. From this they argue VLMs recognize harmful intent but enter a distinct 'jailbreak state' rather than failing to perceive harm. They then define a 'jailbreak direction' d^(l) = normalized(mu_jail - mu_ref) per layer, and a 'jailbreak-related shift' s^(l)(x) = the projection of the image-induced representation shift Delta h^(l)(x) = h([I,T]) - h([empty,T]) onto d^(l). They show this scalar is larger for jailbreak than refusal samples across explicit, implicit, and adversarial-attack scenarios, and correlates with attack success rate (ASR) as harmful visual information or image-text CLIP similarity increases. Building on this, they propose JRS-Rem, a training-free, inference-time defense: at the first generated token, if the normalized shift exceeds a threshold tau=0.2, the jailbreak-related component is subtracted from the hidden state (h_hat = h - s*d).
threat_model: Attacker supplies a multimodal (image + explicitly or implicitly harmful text) input to an open-weight VLM (LLaVA-1.5-7B, ShareGPT4V-7B, InternVL-Chat-19B) to elicit a harmful/policy-violating response (jailbreak). Attack surfaces covered: explicitly harmful text paired with Stable-Diffusion-generated (SD), typographic (TYPO), or SD+TYPO images (HADES, MM-SafetyBench, RedTeam2K datasets); implicitly harmful inputs where harmful intent is conveyed only through the image; and adversarial/geometry- or gradient-based image perturbation attacks (MML-R, MML-M, MML-B64, HADES-gradient). The defense (JRS-Rem) operates purely at inference time on internal hidden representations, requiring only two extra forward passes and a pre-computed per-layer jailbreak direction; it assumes white-box (or at least representation-level) access to the target VLM.
limitations: As stated by the authors: (1) JRS-Rem relies on the pre-existing safety alignment of the VLM's language-model backbone -- if the backbone itself is weakly aligned, the jailbreak-related shift may not be clearly identifiable, limiting effectiveness (untested empirically in the paper). (2) The method specifically targets representation shifts triggered by visual inputs and is not designed to extend to text-only jailbreaks. (3) Verified only up to 19B-parameter models; scalability to significantly larger models is unexplored. Additional grounding-relevant caveats not flagged by the authors but visible in their own results: JRS-Rem's ASR reduction is inconsistent on InternVL-Chat-19B relative to LLaVA/ShareGPT4V (e.g., TYPO on HADES only drops from 24.9 to 13.6, an 11.3-point reduction, much smaller than the 30-70+ point reductions seen elsewhere in the same table), and residual ASR after JRS-Rem is still nontrivial in several cells (e.g., 21.9% on RedTeam2K SD+TYPO for LLaVA-1.5-7B), so the defense reduces but does not eliminate jailbreak susceptibility. The jailbreak direction is computed with a single fixed threshold tau=0.2 chosen empirically per VLM family rather than derived theoretically. The 'safety warning' keyword-based detection (Observation 3) is a heuristic proxy for 'the model recognized harm' and could overcount responses that use hedging language while still being substantively harmful.

datasets_used: HADES (750 explicitly harmful text prompts, 5 harm classes; SD/TYPO/SD+TYPO images; 2,250 multimodal samples)
MM-SafetyBench (1,680 text prompts, 13 harm classes; explicit + implicit versions; 5,040 multimodal samples)
RedTeam2K (2,000 explicitly harmful prompts, 16 policy categories; 6,000 multimodal samples)
HADES (IH) implicit variant using MML harmless rewrites paired with SD+TYPO images (750 samples)
MM-SafetyBench (IH) implicit variant using Rephrased Question field (1,680 samples)
MML adversarial attack set (rotation/mirroring/base64 transforms on HADES images, 2,250 samples)
LLaVA-Instruct-150k subset (3,000 sampled instances) as benign set for representation analysis
MME (2,374 multimodal queries, utility benchmark, max score 2,800)
quantitative_results: Blank image appended to harmful text increases jailbreak success rate of LLaVA-1.5-7B on HADES by 28.13%
Jailbreak responses containing safety warnings: HADES 76.18% (explicit) / 68.52% (implicit); MM-SafetyBench 70.24% / 49.55%; RedTeam2K 69.69% (explicit only), on LLaVA-1.5-7B
LLaVA-1.5-7B baseline ASR on HADES: 77.3 (SD) / 73.7 (TYPO) / 84.9 (SD+TYPO); with JRS-Rem: 12.2 / 9.40 / 12.4 (reductions of 65.1 / 64.3 / 72.5 points)
LLaVA-1.5-7B baseline ASR on MM-SafetyBench: 80.6/79.6/81.4; with JRS-Rem: 12.6/8.00/11.7
LLaVA-1.5-7B baseline ASR on RedTeam2K: 39.3/37.6/49.8; with JRS-Rem: 19.0/18.4/21.9
Implicitly harmful ASR (LLaVA-1.5-7B): HADES 67.0->35.3 with JRS-Rem, MM-SafetyBench 67.2->19.1
Adversarial ASR (LLaVA-1.5-7B): MML-M 67.8->9.0, MML-R 63.7->9.2, MML-B64 68.4->9.6, HADES-gradient 76.1->11.3
Adversarial ASR (ShareGPT4V-7B): MML-M 54.2->4.4, MML-R 63.3->4.1, MML-B64 46.0->4.1, gradient 58.2->2.6
Adversarial ASR (InternVL-Chat-19B): MML-M 24.5->10.4, MML-R 27.7->9.4, MML-B64 30.6->11.8, gradient 31.4->7.6
Utility on LLaVA-1.5-7B with JRS-Rem: MM-Vet 32.1->31.6 (-0.5), ScienceQA 64.0->64.0 (0.0), MME 1754.9->1754.9 (0.0)
Pairwise cosine similarity between 9 jailbreak directions (3 datasets x 3 image types) consistently exceeds 0.7 at layer 19
AUROC of jailbreak direction separating jailbreak vs refusal samples exceeds 0.85 in middle/deep layers for both explicit and implicit scenarios
Fixed threshold tau=0.2 used across all VLMs; jailbreak direction computed from 50 jailbreak + 50 refusal HADES samples
baselines_compared: AdaShield (AdaShield-S variant; prompt-based defense with static human-designed safety instruction)
ECSO (image-to-text captioning defense, safety-check step excluded for fair comparison)
ShiftDC (Zou et al. 2025; removes components along benign-to-harmful direction)
CMRM (Liu et al. 2025; pulls multimodal representations back into text domain)
